      W HY H AS P REDICTING D OWNSTREAM C APABILITIES
002   OF F RONTIER AI M ODELS WITH S CALE R EMAINED

       Anonymous authors

       Paper under double-blind review

010
011                                                A BSTRACT
012
013            Predictable behavior from scaling advanced AI systems is an extremely desirable
014            property for engineers, industry, economists and governments alike, and, while
015            a well-established literature exists on how pretraining performance scales, pre-
016            dictable scaling behavior on downstream capabilities remains elusive. While many
017
               factors are certainly responsible, this paper identifies a significant factor that makes
               predicting scaling behavior on widely used multiple-choice question answering
018
               benchmarks challenging and illuminates a path towards making such downstream
019
               evaluations predictable with scale. Using five model families and twelve well-
020            established multiple-choice benchmarks, we show that downstream performance
021            is computed from negative log likelihoods via a sequence of transformations that
022            progressively degrades the statistical relationship between performance and scale.
023            We then pinpoint the mechanism causing this degradation: downstream metrics
024            require comparing the correct choice against a small number of specific incorrect
025            choices, meaning accurately predicting downstream capabilities requires predict-
026            ing not just how probability mass concentrates on the correct choice with scale,
027            but also how probability mass fluctuates on specific incorrect choices with scale.
028
               We empirically study how probability mass on the correct choice co-varies with
               probability mass on incorrect choices with increasing compute, suggesting that
029
               scaling laws for incorrect choices might be achievable. Our work also explains
030
               why pretraining scaling laws are commonly regarded as more predictable than
031            downstream capabilities and contributes towards establishing scaling-predictable
032            evaluations of frontier AI models.
033
034
035   1    T HE I MPORTANCE OF P REDICTING C APABILITIES WITH S CALE
036
037   Predictable scaling behavior of frontier AI systems such as GPT-4 (OpenAI, 2024; OpenAI et al.,
038   2024), Claude (Anthropic, 2024) and Gemini (Team et al., 2023; Reid et al., 2024) is crucial for
039   anticipating capabilities and informing key decisions regarding model development and deployment
040   (Anthropic, 2023; OpenAI, 2023; Dragan et al., 2024). Predictable scaling behaviors enable engineers
041   to make informed decisions about optimal model design choices and to de-risk investment in exceed-
042   ingly expensive pretraining runs by determining the payoff from scaling up compute. For instance,
043   OpenAI noted in the GPT-4 Technical Report (Achiam et al., 2023) that “A large focus of the GPT-4
044   project was building a deep learning stack that scales predictably” and that “[OpenAI] developed
045
      infrastructure and optimization methods that have very predictable behavior across multiple scales”;
      OpenAI noted that this ideally goes beyond predicting loss values, and that “Having a sense of the
046
      capabilities of a model before training can improve decisions around alignment, safety, and deploy-
047
      ment”. Meta’s Llama Team similarly conducted experiments aimed at predicting the downstream
048   performance of models, used to inform the design of their 405 billion parameter model (Dubey et al.,
049   2024). Additionally, predicting capabilities is of interest beyond AI practitioners: economists and
050   governments also have a significant interest in predicting the capabilities of current and future frontier
051   AI systems for better decision-making (regulation, taxation, and safety) and forecasting of economic
052   impacts (Council of Economic Advisers, 2024). Downstream capabilities are especially of interest
053   because quantities like pretraining loss are difficult to translate into quantities more meaningful to
      society, such as the impact on economic labor or societal harms.


                                                          1
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


054
055
056
057
058
059
060
061
062
063
064
065
      Figure 1: Multiple-choice benchmark accuracy is computed from negative log likelihoods via a
066
      sequence of transformations that degrades predictability. Computing Accuracy begins with
067
      computing the negative log likelihoods of each choice, then negating and exponentiating each to
068   obtain the probability of each choice (A). Choices are then restricted to a set of available choices by
069   masking invalid continuations, and renormalizing to obtain relative probability mass on each choice
070   (B). Lastly, the model’s choice is defined as arg maxi {pChoices (Available Choicei )}, and Accuracy
071   is 1 if and only if the model’s choice is the correct choice (C).
072
073
074
      However, while scaling laws describing relationships amongst parameters, data, compute, and
      pretraining loss are well-established (Hestness et al., 2017; Rosenfeld et al., 2019; Henighan et al.,
075
      2020; Kaplan et al., 2020; Gordon et al., 2021; Hernandez et al., 2021; Jones, 2021; Zhai et al., 2022;
076
      Hoffmann et al., 2022; Clark et al., 2022; Neumann & Gros, 2022; Hernandez et al., 2022; Maloney
077   et al., 2022; Sardana & Frankle, 2023; Muennighoff et al., 2024; Besiroglu et al., 2024), the literature
078   is less conclusive regarding predicting specific downstream capabilities with scale. For instance, prior
079   work has observed that performance on standard natural language processing (NLP) benchmarks can
080   exhibit emergent abilities (Brown et al., 2020; Ganguli et al., 2022; Srivastava et al., 2022; Wei et al.,
081   2022) where performance changes unpredictably with scale, but further work demonstrated that such
082   unpredictable changes might at times be artifacts of researchers’ analyses, i.e., choices of metrics and
083   lack of sufficient resolution from too few samples (Srivastava et al., 2022; Schaeffer et al., 2023; Hu
084   et al., 2024). More recently, Du et al. (2024) claim that downstream capabilities can be predicted, but
085
      only after the pretraining cross-entropy loss falls below a certain threshold, and Gadre et al. (2024)
      claim that while performance on individual tasks can be difficult to predict, aggregating results across
086
      dozens of diverse benchmarks yields clearer scaling trends. In this work, we ask: in contrast with
087
      strongly-predictable pretraining losses, why has predicting specific downstream capabilities with
088   scale remained elusive?
089
090
091
      2    C ONTRIBUTION : E XPLAINING W HY P REDICTING D OWNSTREAM
092        C APABILITIES W ITH S CALE H AS R EMAINED E LUSIVE
093
094   Our goal is to understand what breaks down between the predictability of pretraining losses and the
095   unpredictability of downstream evaluations. To do this, we investigated the relative predictability
096
      of different evaluation methodologies and setups, focusing on popular and comparatively simple
      (yet still highly difficult to predict) multiple-choice question answering benchmarks. We began with
097
      scaling-predictable pretraining log likelihoods and tracked how these log likelihoods are transformed
098
      in the process of calculating downstream evaluation metrics that are notoriously difficult to predict,
099   such as Accuracy or Brier Score (See Fig. 1 and Sec. 4 for further detail):
100
101
102   log pVocab
           θ     (Correct Choice) → pVocab
                                     θ     (Correct Choice) → pChoices
                                                               θ       (Correct Choice) →         Accuracy
                                                                                                  | {z   }
103
      |            {z           }
            Scaling-Predictable                                                                 Scaling-Unpredictable
104
105   This paper will demonstrate the following summary of our findings:
106
107         1. Calculating downstream metrics requires a sequence of transformations applied to
               the original scaling-predictable quantities. These transformations progressively dete-


                                                          2
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


108
                  riorate the statistical relationship between those metrics and the scaling parameters
109               (parameters, data, and compute). This formalizes an intuition that “more complex”
110               metrics might be less easily predictable.
111
               2. Accurately predicting downstream multiple-choice performance requires modeling
112
                  not only the probability mass assigned to the correct choice with scale, but also the
113               probability mass assigned to the incorrect alternatives. This explains the cause of
114               comparative unpredictability of multiple-choice benchmarks, and also suggests a potential
115               path forward for successful predictive models of downstream performance in the area of
116               multiple-choice question answering, and in general the need to model external information
117               not related to scaling-predictable log likelihoods needed for downstream metric computation.
118            3. Continuous metrics such as Brier Score are insufficient for recovering predictabil-
119               ity. We observe that, contrary to prior work showing that metrics such as Brier Score
120               can hide emergent behavior at times, Brier Score is insufficient to improve the statistical
121               relationship degraded by incorporating incorrect choices’ probability mass.
122
123   Our findings explain that the apparent unpredictability of individual downstream evaluations is due to
124   specific incorrect choices, which the strongly predictable pretraining losses do not depend upon. More
125
      broadly, we argue that a precise understanding of the factors affecting downstream performance is
      essential for designing evaluations to reliably track the progression of frontier AI models’ capabilities.
126
127
128   3       M ETHODOLOGY: DATA FOR S TUDYING S CALING OF D OWNSTREAM
129           C APABILITIES
130
131   To study how downstream capabilities on specific tasks change with scale for different model families,
132   we generated per-sample scores from a large number of model families and multiple-choice NLP
133   benchmarks. To ensure the computed scores were consistent with prior work, we used EleutherAI’s
134   Language Model Evaluation Harness (Gao et al., 2023).
135
136   Model Families Because our goal is to explore the scaling behavior of evaluations with increasing
137   compute, we chose to evaluate model families with dense combinations of parameter counts and
138
      token counts. The following families were evaluated (additional details in App. D):
139
               1. Pythia (Biderman et al., 2023b): The Pythia family contains 8 models from 70M to 12B
140
                  parameters trained on the Pile (Gao et al., 2020) for 300B tokens. We used 8 checkpoints
141               per size of the non-deduplicated variants.
142
               2. Cerebras-GPT (Dey et al., 2023): The Cerebras-GPT family contains 7 models ranging
143
                  from 111M to 13B parameters. The models were trained on the Pile (Gao et al., 2020) for
144
                  different durations as part of a scaling study with a ratio of ∼ 20× tokens to parameters in a
145               “Chinchilla”-optimal manner (Hoffmann et al., 2022).
146
               3. OLMo (Groeneveld et al., 2024): The OLMo family contains a 1B parameter model trained
147
                  for 3T tokens and two 7B parameter models trained for 2T-2.5T tokens. We selected 7
148
                  checkpoints for 1B (spanning 84B1 to 3T tokens) and 7 checkpoints for 7B (spanning 4B to
149               2.4T tokens).
150
               4. INCITE (AI, 2023): The INCITE family contains 3B and 7B parameter models, trained on
151
                  0.8T and 1T tokens of RedPajama-v1(Computer, 2023). The 3B model has only a single
152
                  checkpoint, so we excluded it. We found this family to be a slight outlier from other families,
153               which we speculate is because its pretraining data were contaminated by benchmarks (Elazar
154               et al., 2023).
155
               5. LLM360 (Liu et al., 2023): LLM360 includes two 7B parameter LLMs trained on 1.3T and
156
                  1.4T tokens. We selected 13 checkpoints of Amber spaced approximately logarithmically.
157
158
      NLP Benchmarks We evaluated the above model families on widely-used multiple-choice bench-
159   marks for assessing comprehension, reasoning, and world knowledge: AI2 Reasoning Challenge
160   (ARC) Easy and Hard (Clark et al., 2018), HellaSwag (Zellers et al., 2019), MathQA (Amini et al.,
161

              OLMo 1B checkpoints below 84B tokens were unfortunately accidentally lost by their creators.

200
      Performance Metrics We used three common multiple-choice metrics (Srivastava et al., 2022;
201
      Schaeffer et al., 2023; Du et al., 2024): Accuracy, Brier Score (Brier, 1950), and probability
202   mass on the correct choice relative to the available choices pChoices (Correct Choice).
                                                                    θ
203
204
      Compute Budget Calculations Following prior work (Kaplan et al., 2020), we approximated2
205   the pretraining compute C (in terms of training FLOP) of a given model checkpoint as a function of
206   the parameter count (excluding embeddings) N and the amount of training data seen in tokens D:
207   C = C(N, D) ≈ 6 N D.

      quantities are computed via a sequence of transformations that begins with the negative log likelihood
253   of the correct choice on this particular benchmark sample as some function f (·, ·) of compute:
254
255
256                                           LVocab
                                               θ     (Correct Choice) = f (Compute, Benchmark Datum)                                                                                         (1)
257
      Two details are critical. Firstly, this negative log likelihood is not computed in expectation over a
258
      corpus; it is specific to this particular singular datum in the benchmark. All the scores we discuss are
259   per-datum. Secondly, this negative log likelihood is computed over the vocabulary of the model. One
260   can then compute the probability mass of the correct choice, again with respect to the vocabulary:
261
262
                                               pVocab (Correct Choice) = exp − LVocab

                                                θ                               θ     (Correct Choice)                                                                                       (2)

      We distinguish the support over the token space of the model versus over the set of available choices
271   in the benchmark’s question because, as we will show, the support crucially affects predictability.
272   Finally, the choices-normalized probability masses become standard downstream metrics:
273

                Accuracyθ = 1 Correct Choice == arg max pChoices

279
280   where 1(·) is an indicator variable. To quantify how this sequence of transformations affects pre-
281   dictability of performance, we measured how per-sample scores correlate with pretraining compute,
282   and then studied how the distribution (over samples) of correlation values shifts from log likelihoods
283   to pVocab
           θ    (Correct Choice) to pChoices
                                       θ     (Correct Choice) to Accuracy or Brier Score. Specifi-
284
      cally, for each combination of (model family, benchmark, performance metric, correlation metric),
      we computed a correlation value for each sample in the benchmark between pretraining compute and
285
      scores. This yielded a distribution (over samples) of correlation values for the combination (Fig. 2
286
      Left). Visualizing the distribution of correlations for the combination told us what fraction of samples
287   in the benchmark yielded scores that are correlated, uncorrelated or anticorrelated with compute (Fig.
288   2 Right). We found consistent results using all three standard correlation metrics: Pearson (1895),
289   Kendall (1938) and Spearman (1961).
290
      We demonstrate how the sequence of transformations affects the distribution of score-compute
291
      correlations using ARC Challenge (Clark et al., 2018) as an illustrative benchmark; we note that all
292
      other benchmarks exhibited similar patterns as well (App. G). We visualized the distributions via
293   their complementary (empirical) cumulative distribution functions (complementary CDFs) (App. B):
294
                                                               S
295                                                         1X
                                               Ŝ(c) =
                                                      def
                                                                  1{Cs > c},                                          (6)
296                                                         S s=1
297
      where S is the number of samples in the benchmark and Cs is the correlation (over the models in the
298
      model family) between compute and scores on the s-th sample in the benchmark. For a given threshold
299
      c, the complementary CDF Ŝ(c) returns the fraction of the benchmark’s samples with score-compute
300
      correlations greater than the threshold c (Fig. 3A). Beginning with log likelihoods, approximately
301
      90% of samples exhibit score-compute correlations > 0.75, regardless of the model family (Fig.
302   3A). Transforming negative log likelihoods into probability masses pVocab        (Correct Choice) does
                                                                                  θ
303   not affect the distribution of score-compute correlations for Spearman and Kendall. However,
304   transforming pVocab
                      θ   (Correct Choice) into pChoices
                                                 θ       (Correct Choice) decreases the distribution of score-
305   compute correlations (Fig. 3B), with only 40% of samples having score-compute correlations
306   > 0.75. Transforming pChoices
                               θ     (Correct Choice) into Brier Score has little-to-no effect (Fig. 3C),
307   but transforming into Accuracy (Fig. 3D) furthers decreases score-compute correlations. To
308   quantitatively test whether these transformations indeed decrease the correlation between scores and
309   compute, we measured four statistics of these score-compute correlation distributions: (1) the mean,
310
      (2) the median, (3) the area under the complementary CDF and (4) the negative3 of the minimum of
      two Wasserstein distances: between the empirical correlation distribution and an ideal distribution of
311
      all correlations = 1, and between the empirical distribution and an ideal distribution of all correlations
312
      = −1. Across the four summary statistics, for most benchmarks and for most model families, we
313   discovered a consistent ordering of metrics of the score-compute correlation distributions (Fig. 4):
314

      Figure 4: All four statistics of score-compute correlation distributions demonstrate that trans-
347   forming log pVocab   (Correct Choice) into Accuracy causes score-compute correlations to dete-
                      θ
348   riorate. We find a consistent trend that the sequence of transformations degrades score-compute
349   correlations, as shown by the right-to-left log pVocab

                                                                                      θ    (Correct Choice)-
           Choices
350   to-pθ        (Correct Choice)-or-Brier Score-to-Accuracy vertical stripes. This trend holds

357   To quantitatively confirm that the correlation scores indeed follow this ordering, we computed what
358
      fraction of (benchmark, correlation metric, model family, correlation distribution statistic) tuples obey
      the ordering. To be maximally conservative, we checked for strict inequalities only. We found that
359
      across benchmarks, model families, and the 4 correlation distribution statistics, the claimed ordering
360
      of metrics held at least 82.4% of the time for Pearson, 85.6% for Spearman and 90.4% for Kendall.
361
362
363   5                                       P ROBABILITY M ASS ON I NCORRECT C HOICES C AUSES U NPREDICTABILITY
364
365   What is the mechanism that degrades how correlated scores are with compute? All three metrics
366   with degraded correlations - pChoices
                                      θ     (Correct Choice), Accuracy, and Brier Score - depend
367   not just on how the model’s probability mass pVocab
                                                        θ   (Correct Choice) concentrates on the correct
368   choice as compute increases, but also depend on how the model’s probability mass fluctuates on
369   incorrect available choices {pVocab
                                      θ    (Incorrect Choice)}Incorrect Choices as compute increases. As an
                          V ocab
370   example, suppose pθ        (Correct Choice) = 0.4 on a 4-way multiple-choice question; what is the
371   accuracy? Spreading the remaining mass uniformly on the incorrect choices will make Accuracy
372   = 1, whereas concentrating mass on a single incorrect choice will make Accuracy = 0.
373   To demonstrate how drastically the probability mass placed on incorrect choices can alter performance,
374   we visualized the relationships between pairs of metrics immediately preceding and following a given
375   transformation (Fig. 5). For negative log likelihood of the correct choice and pVθ ocab (Correct Choice)
376   (not pictured), we observed a clean correspondence between performance under the metric and
377   compute: one can reliably map a given value of these metrics to compute, and vice versa. In
      contrast, once performance is evaluated using a metric that is a function of the incorrect choices


                                                                                                                                         7
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

408
409   Figure 5: Predictability deteriorates because of probability mass fluctuating on specific incorrect
410   choices with scale. Left: Transitioning from pVocab   θ   (Correct Choice) to pChoices
                                                                                     θ       (Correct Choice)
411   demonstrates that pθ (Correct Choice) contains little information about pChoices
                           Vocab
                                                                                     θ       (Correct Choice)
412   and vice versa; loosely speaking, any value of one can map to any value of the other. Center: While
413   pChoices
       θ       (Correct Choice) > 0.5 must yield Accuracy = 1, for any pChoicesθ    (Correct Choice) < 0.5,
414   knowing pChoices
                  θ     (Correct Choice) contains little information about Accuracy    and vice versa. Right:
415   Brier Score is more predictable from pChoices  θ      (Correct Choice) than Accuracy,     but still quite
416   variable. Three benchmarks shown: MathQA Amini et al. (2019), MMLU Conceptual Physics
417   Hendrycks et al. (2020), SciQ Welbl et al. (2017).
418
419
      - pChoices
         θ       (Correct Choice), Accuracy or Brier Score - nearly any value of a score under one
420
      metric can map to any value of pVocab
                                         θ   (Correct Choice) or pChoices
                                                                    θ     (Correct Choice) respectively (Fig.
421
      5), thereby breaking the chain along which one can cleanly infer compute from an observed metric.
422
      We can see that Brier Score, a metric meant to produce more continuous scores (Schaeffer et al.,
423   2023), is less variable than Accuracy, provided a known pChoices     (Correct Choice), but it cannot re-
                                                                      θ
424   cover information about pVocab  (Correct Choice) that is lost when shifting to pChoices (Correct Choice).
                                 θ                                                    θ
425   We next show that this is because of the additional information regarding the underdetermined values
426   of pChoices
           θ      (Incorrect Choice) for each incorrect choice.
427
428
429   6    S CALING B EHAVIOR OF P ROBABILITY M ASS ON I NCORRECT C HOICES
430
431   In general, aggregate performance over a distribution is often of interest. Such a focus on aggregate
      performance leads to an important insight: in MCQA, probability mass fluctuations on incorrect


                                                         8
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


432
433
434
435
436
437
438
439
440
441
442
443
444
445
446
447
448
449
450
451
452
453
454
455
456
457
458
459
460
461
462   Figure 6: Probability mass on the correct choices and the incorrect choices are correlated, but
463   can fluctuate substantially. Probability mass on correct choices and incorrect choices positively
464   covaries and typically increases with compute. However, the spread is large: for any given value of
465   pVocab
       θ     (Correct Choice), the mass on incorrect choices can vary by many orders of magnitude.
466
467
468   choices do not “average out". Unlike estimating the mean of a random variable, where positive and
469   negative deviations cancel, the nonlinear nature of metrics like Accuracy and Brier Score means that
470   probability mass shifts between incorrect options affect scores in complex ways that persist under
471
      averaging. For example, if probability mass shifts from incorrect option A to incorrect option B, the
      impact on accuracy depends on whether either option had enough mass to compete with the correct
472
      answer - there’s no natural cancellation. This perhaps counter-intuitive behavior partially explains
473
      why predicting aggregate performance using typical averaging has remained elusive.
474
475   This analysis suggests that modeling probability mass fluctuations on incorrect choices could improve
476   predictions of metrics like Accuracy and Brier Score, though the magnitude of improvement remains
477
      an open question. For metrics like Accuracy, such predictions should be made for each sample
      because knowing the average mass (across many data) placed on incorrect choices says little about
478
      how much mass is placed on any single incorrect choice for a single sample. We conclude by
479
      providing preliminary evidence that achieving such a feat might be possible. Specifically, we test
480   how probability masses on correct choices and probability masses on incorrect choices covary with
481   increasing compute (Fig. 6). Multiple benchmarks display strong positive relationships between mass
482   on correct choices and mass on incorrect choices, suggesting that fitting per-sample scaling trends
483   for each incorrect choice might be possible; doing so might enable better predicting changepoints
484   in metrics like Accuracy or Brier Score. However, whether per-benchmark per-sample per-
485   choice scaling trends can be fit and accurately extrapolated is unclear since the spread varies by
      several orders of magnitude. We leave this challenge to future work.


                                                       9
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


486
487       Takeaway #1: Think through your metrics!
488
          If one cares about scaling-predictable evaluations, then one needs to think through how their
          evaluations transform raw model outputs into useful signals to know what to expect.
489
490       Takeaway #2: Continuous metrics are insufficient to guarantee predictable changes.
491       As shown by pChoices
                         θ      (Correct Choice) & Brier Score, even “continuous” metrics can be
492       unpredictable, e.g., if the metric weighs correct behavior against specific incorrect behaviors.
493       Takeaway #3: Recommended scaling-predictable metrics for pretraining practitioners.
494       Pretraining practitioners seeking scaling-predictable signals for capabilities are advised to
495       focus on pVocab
                    θ     (Correct Choice) on relevant benchmarks. Scores under this metric provide
496       smoother scaling trends and are arguably more interpretable than the pretraining loss.
497
          Takeaway #4: Evaluations should be reshaped based on intended desiderata.
498       Too often, we take evaluations as frozen static objects, but evaluations should be adapted
499       to pertinent goals. For instance, if the goal is to predict capabilities with scale, evaluations
500       should be designed or adapted to be scaling-predictable.
501
502
503
504   7    D ISCUSSION , R ELATED W ORK AND F UTURE D IRECTIONS
505
506   This work identifies a factor that induces unpredictability in multiple-choice assessments of frontier AI
507
      models, as well as the underlying mechanism: probability mass on incorrect choices. Our results have
      implications for the design of future evaluations of frontier AI models that are reliably predictable
508
      with scaling. We hope that our work will be extended to further the science of scaling-predictable
509
      evaluation of AI systems, especially for complex and important model capabilities. We note several
510   future directions for extension of our work, and we hope that the community also adopts our framing
511   to further improve scaling-predictable evaluations.
512
513   Related Work We intentionally wove key related work into our main text, with a particular
514   emphasis in the Introdution. For a longer and more comprehensive exposition, see Appendix A.
515
516   Direction 1: Beyond Multiple Choice Benchmarks Our study is restricted to benchmarks evalu-
517   ated via log likelihood-based multiple-choice formats. While we believe this is inherently valuable
518   due to the usefulness and prevalence of such tasks, this limits the application of our findings. We hope
519   that our discoveries and proposed mechanisms may be used to inform the study of predictable and
520   reliable evaluation writ large, and that future work should explore the extent to which our findings
521
      can be generalized to more complex capabilities. Our findings corroborate those of Lyu et al. (2024),
      who find that multiple-choice answer scores often diverge from generative evaluations. Consequently,
522
      a particularly important direction for further study is to investigate generative evaluations, which may
523
      contain similar transformations distancing performance from the observed loss.
524
525
      Direction 2: Predicting Benchmark Performance A Priori Our work provides an explanation
526   why multiple-choice benchmark performance is not easily predictable for metrics such as Accuracy
527   and Brier Score, as observed in the literature (Du et al., 2024). However, our analyses assume
528   access to entire model families’ scores across several orders of magnitude of pretraining FLOPs, and
529   do not employ backtesting, as sensibly recommended by (Alabdulmohsin et al., 2022; Owen, 2024).
530   A predictive model should be able to identify change points well in advance on standard metrics like
531   Accuracy or Brier Score.
532

                                                     18
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


972
       A    R ELATED W ORK
973
974
       Language Model Evaluation The capabilities of AI models are typically evaluated using con-
975
       structed datasets to assess performance on a specific task, acting as a proxy for some real-world usage
976    scenario. However, performing robust and reliable evaluations is a challenge, with many potential
977    pitfalls and unsolved problems (Biderman et al., 2024). For example, we might prefer to ask models
978    open-ended questions and evaluate their answers in natural language, but it then often becomes
979    difficult to robustly score the resulting model outputs, especially for partial correctness. For this
980    reason, it is common practice for evaluation benchmarks to simplify their scoring via approximations,
981    such as extracting a sub-string from free-form outputs heuristically (Joshi et al., 2017; Kwiatkowski
982    et al., 2019; Hendrycks et al., 2021) and checking that it matches a specific gold target string, or
983    casting a task to a multiple-choice format, in which a closed set of correct and incorrect answers
984
       is known, and the model’s answer is determined by selecting the most likely option among these
       strings. For more details on the precise procedures typically used for multiple choice elsewhere in the
985
       literature, see Biderman et al. (2024). We believe that the multiple-choice format is valuable, due to
986
       its flexibility, popularity and relevance (Brown et al., 2020; Beeching et al., 2023; Biderman et al.,
987    2024), but we discuss its limitations in Section 7.
988
989
       Scaling Laws Many neural networks exhibit power-law scaling of the pretraining loss as a function
990
       of the amount of compute, data, or parameters used for training (Hestness et al., 2017; Brown et al.,
991    2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022). These neural scaling laws demonstrate that the pretraining loss can
992    be highly predictable as a function of these fundamental inputs, which has a number of practical
993    applications: Scaling laws fit to smaller training runs can be used to predict the pretraining loss of
994    a much larger training run, and can be used to determine effective hyperparameters (McCandlish
995    et al., 2018; DeepSeek-AI et al., 2024), or the optimal allocation of dataset and model size for a
996    given compute budget (Hoffmann et al., 2022; Muennighoff et al., 2024; Dey et al., 2023; Sardana &
997    Frankle, 2023; Besiroglu et al., 2024). In some cases, such laws can be used to predict performance of
998    a larger model in a particular domain, such as coding (Achiam et al., 2023). The existence of scaling
999
       laws turns deep learning into a predictable science at the macro level by providing a simple recipe for
       improving model quality and de-risking returns on increasing investment into scale (Ganguli et al.,
1000
       2022; Bowman, 2023).
1001
1002
       Emergent Abilities Language models have been observed to exhibit apparent emergent abilities—
1003
       behaviors on downstream task performance that cannot be predicted from smaller scales (Wei et al.,
1004
       2022; Srivastava et al., 2022). Emergence appears not to be simply a product of training compute
1005   or model size, but is also dependent on other factors such as dataset composition (Muckatira et al.,
1006   2024; Wei et al., 2022). Schaeffer et al. (2023) find that some emergent phenomena can be a “mirage”
1007   arising due to choices made by researchers such as the use of discontinuous metrics and insufficient
1008   resolution. However, Du et al. (2024) note that for many tasks, emergence remains despite the use
1009   of continuous metrics. Additionally, discontinuous metrics have been argued to often be the most
1010   reflective of real-world usefulness, so emergence in these hard metrics is important. Hu et al. (2024)
1011   found that for generative evaluations, infinite resolution can be achieved but requires significant
1012   compute and that generated answer be verifiable.
1013
1014   Predicting Downstream Task Performance Although predicting macroscopic pretraining loss is
1015   useful, a far more useful goal is to predict the scaling of model performance on particular downstream
1016   tasks or domains. If this was possible, then model developers could tune their datasets and training
1017
       procedures in a more fine-grained way before launching computationally intensive training runs.
       Model performance on a particular downstream task is typically correlated with compute, albeit
1018
       with a few exceptions (McKenzie et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2024). However, despite attempts to fit
1019
       scaling laws to values other than loss, including benchmark scores (Gadre et al., 2024; Zhang et al.,
1020   2024), model memorization (Biderman et al., 2023a), or reward (Gao et al., 2022), these downstream
1021   performance metrics are usually more noisy or require more compute to fit accurately. Owen (2024)
1022   and Gadre et al. (2024) both find that while aggregate benchmark performance with more compute
1023   can be predicted, the scaling behaviour of individual tasks can be noisy. Additionally, Owen (2024),
1024   Du et al. (2024) and Gadre et al. (2024) claim that predicting scaling behavior on a task without
1025   access to models exhibiting better-than-random performance (i.e., “before emergence occurs”) cannot
       be done reliably. Concurrently to our work, Ruan et al. (2024) propose Observational Scaling Laws


                                                        19
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1026
       by mapping model capabilities from compute to a shared low-dimensional space of capabilities across
1027   model families before predicting performance on novel tasks. Our goal in this work is to investigate
1028   the comparative unpredictability of individual downstream performance scores, and advise how to
1029   create more scaling-predictable evaluations that are closely coupled with real-world use-cases.
1030
1031
1032
       B    D EFINITION OF S URVIVAL F UNCTION
1033
       The survival function SX (x) – also known as the reliability function, the tail distribution, or the
1034
       complementary cumulative distribution function – gives the probability that a random variable X
1035   exceeds a certain value x Kleinbaum & Klein (2012); contributors (2023):
1036                                                Z ∞
                                    def
1037                         SX (x) = P r[X > x] =       fX (x′ ) dx′ = 1 − FX (x)                       (7)
1038                                                    x

1039   where FX (x) = P r[X ≤ x] is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) and fX (x) is the
1040   probability density function (pdf) or probability mass function (pmf) of the random variable X. The
1041
       CDF FX (x) gives the probability that the random variable X is at most x, while the survival function
       SX (x) gives the probability that X exceeds x.
1042
1043   When the true distribution of X is unknown, we can use the empirical CDF (ECDF) F̂X (x) and the
1044   empirical survival function (ESF) ŜX (x):
1045                                               n
                                            def 1
                                                  X
1046                                ŜX (x) =         1{xi > x} = 1 − F̂X (x)                      (8)
1047                                            n i=1
1048   where n is the number of observations, xi is the realized value of the random variable X for
1049   observation i, and 1{xi > x} is the indicator function. The empirical survival function ŜX (x)
1050   specifies the fraction of observations for which the sampled random variable X exceeds x.
1051
1052
       C    C OMPUTE R ESOURCES FOR E XPERIMENTS
1053
1054   Experiments were done across a wide family of model families and sizes. The GPUs we used for
1055   medium-sized models (7B parameters and above) used a single A100s with 80GB of vRAM. For
1056   smaller models (≤8B) we used A100s with 80GB of vRAM, Quadro RTX 8000 with 48GB of vRAM,
1057   or RTX A4000 with 16GB of vRAM. For 70B parameter models, we used at least 2 A100 GPUs with
1058   80GB of vRAM.
1059
1060
       D    A DDITIONAL M ODEL FAMILY D ETAILS
1061
1062   Here we provide further experimental details regarding our selection of model families.
1063
1064         1. Pythia (Biderman et al., 2023b): We consider two “families” for Pythia in our experiments.
1065            Pythia (Parameter Scaling) refers to the use of fully-trained checkpoints from 9 different
1066            model sizes (all model sizes documented in Biderman et al. (2023), as well as a 14M
1067            parameter model trained later by the authors). Pythia-12B (Data Scaling) refers to the use
1068
                of 8 checkpoints across training for the Pythia-12B model, namely having seen 2M, 64M,
                2B, 6B, 20B, 60B, 200B, and 300B tokens in training.
1069
1070         2. Cerebras-GPT (Dey et al., 2023): Cerebras (Parameter and Data Scaling) refers to our
1071            use of 1 checkpoint per model in the Cerebras-GPT family, each fully trained for differing
1072
                quantities of data as documented by the model creators, for 7 checkpoints in total.
1073         3. OLMo (Groeneveld et al., 2024): OLMo (7B Data Scaling) refers to the use of 7 check-
1074            points for OLMo-7B across training, namely, checkpoints having seen 4B, 44B, 133B, 442B,
1075
                885B, 1.5T, and 2.4T tokens.
1076         4. INCITE (AI, 2023): INCITE-7B (Data Scaling) considers 6 checkpoints over training for
1077            the 7B parameter model, having seen 240B, 280B, 400B, 500B, 700B, and 1T tokens.
1078         5. LLM360 (Liu et al., 2023): LLM360 Amber (Data Scaling) considers 13 checkpoints of
1079            the Amber model, having seen 0B, 3.5B, 7B, 10.5B, 17.5B, 31.5B, 49B, 87.5B, 147B, 252B,
                430B, 738B, and 1.26T tokens.


                                                        20
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1080
       E    B ROADER I MPACT
1081
1082
       This paper contributes to a better understanding of the predictability of large language models
1083   (LLMs), which can have both positive and negative societal impacts. On the positive side, by making
1084   LLM benchmarks more predictable, this research can help society anticipate and plan for potential
1085   challenges associated with their development and deployment. This increased predictability can
1086   facilitate proactive measures to mitigate risks and ensure the responsible use of AI technologies.
1087
       However, the increased predictability of LLMs could theoretically be exploited by malicious actors
1088
       to accelerate the development of AI systems designed for malicious purposes. We also stress the
1089   importance of proactive risk assessment and the implementation of safeguards to prevent the misuse
1090   of AI technologies.
1091
